cards.text: The Sultan of Selangor's pig-farming ban took effect not on his decree date but on 12 February 2026, when the State Executive Council formally adopted it.
cards.sub: Article 71 of the Federal Constitution vests state executive authority in the Ruler. The MMKN's adoption was the lawful conversion from titah to operative state policy.
sources: Federal Constitution Article 71 + Eighth Schedule (AGC reprint, 1 Nov 2010)
Laws of the Constitution of Selangor 1959, Article 50
Bernama, Sultan Of Selangor Sets Three Policy Directives For State Pig Farming Operations
Business Today, 12 Feb 2026, Selangor Decides To Stop Issuing Pig Farm Licences
CodeBlue, Feb 2026, MB: Selangor To Completely Import Pork, Ban Pig Farming

cards.h: Article 71 and the Eighth Schedule require every state constitution to vest the Ruler with executive authority. The Selangor case ran the route end to end.
cards.s: Sultan Sharafuddin issued three escalating decrees from 12 January to 7 May 2026. State Exco adopted the ban on 12 February. Licensing stopped that day.
cards.d: Article 71 of the Federal Constitution guarantees each Ruler the constitutional rights and privileges of Ruler of his state, and the Eighth Schedule's 'essential provisions' require every state constitution to vest the state's executive authority in the Ruler, exercisable on Executive Council advice for non-discretionary matters. Article 50 of the Laws of the Constitution of Selangor 1959 implements this directly. The pig-farming sequence ran through the route in three steps: the Sultan's decree of 12 January 2026 (no more pig farming in Tanjong Sepat, relocation to Bukit Tagar), revised on 10 February 2026 to a total state ban with pork imports as the alternative; the State Executive Council's formal adoption on 12 February 2026, which also stopped licence issuance; and the 7 May 2026 Royal Office reaffirmation framed as a 'full stop' to debate. Bernama records the Sultan's three policy directives as primary government wire. The pathway is what the constitution prescribes for a State List matter (Ninth Schedule items including land use and licensing of trades).
sources: Federal Constitution Article 71 + Eighth Schedule
Laws of the Constitution of Selangor 1959, Article 50
The Star, 12 Jan 2026, No more pig farms in Tanjong Sepat, decrees Selangor Ruler
Malay Mail, 10 Feb 2026, Sultan Sharafuddin says no to pig farming in Selangor
Bernama, Sultan Of Selangor Sets Three Policy Directives
Business Today, 12 Feb 2026, Selangor Decides To Stop Issuing Pig Farm Licences
Malay Mail, 7 May 2026, Selangor Royal Office: Sultan decrees 'full stop'

cards.h: On 16 May 2026 about 5,000 attended a Daulat Tuanku rally in Shah Alam. The same day, PUTRA's Ibrahim Ali publicly proposed an Akta Kedaulatan Raja.
cards.s: Reported provisions include detention without warrant for up to two weeks and preventive detention up to two years, plus a Royal Sovereignty Council. The proposal is addressed to government; it is not a Cabinet bill.
cards.d: The rally, organised by PAS Selangor Youth, drew an FMT-reported 5,000 attendees (Malaysiakini headline reports 3,000+) at the Sultan Salahuddin Abdul Aziz Shah Mosque before marching to the Selangor State Secretariat. Coalition: PAS, UMNO, Bersatu, Pejuang, PUTRA, PERKASA, and about 300 Malay NGOs. Selangor PAS Commissioner Ab Halim Tamuri and Selangor UMNO chief Megat Zulkarnain Omardin attended. On the same day, PUTRA party president Ibrahim Ali proposed the Akta Kedaulatan Raja (Royal Sovereignty Act) in public statements covered by FMT, Malaysiakini, Kosmo, Harakah Daily and Sinar Harian. The stated rationale: existing laws are inadequate against insults, treason and provocation against the royal institution and the position of Islam. The proposal would supplement, not replace, the existing protective stack — Sedition Act 1948 s.3(1)(f), Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 s.233, Penal Code ss.298, 298A, 504. The bill is at proposal stage only; no draft text exists, the Cabinet has not endorsed it, and the Conference of Rulers has not issued a public statement on it as of brief close.
sources: FMT, 16 May 2026, Some 5,000 attend rally in solidarity with Selangor sultan
FMT (bahasa), 16 May 2026, 5,000 sertai Himpunan Daulat Tuanku di Shah Alam
Malaysiakini, 16 May 2026, Daulat Tuanku rally: Reject DAP in GE16
FMT, 16 May 2026, Ibrahim Ali calls for ISA-like law to protect royalty from insults
Malaysiakini, 16 May 2026, Putra moots harsher than ISA law to defend royal institution
Harakah Daily, 17 May 2026, PUTRA Selangor gesa RUU Akta Kedaulatan Raja digubal
Kosmo Digital, 16 May 2026, Gubal RUU Akta Kedaulatan Raja hukum pihak cemar institusi Diraja

cards.h: Article 181 of the Federal Constitution already preserves the Rulers' sovereignty, and Sedition Act Section 3(1)(f) makes questioning it a seditious tendency.
cards.s: Lèse-majesté statutes exist in Thailand (Penal Code s.112, 3 to 15 years), Spain, Denmark, the Netherlands and Cambodia. Each constitutional monarchy strikes the balance with free expression differently.
cards.d: Article 181 of the Federal Constitution states that the sovereignty, prerogatives, powers and jurisdiction of the Rulers within their respective territories 'as hitherto had and enjoyed shall remain unaffected,' subject to the rest of the constitution. The Sedition Act 1948 Section 3(1)(f) directly anchors in this article, treating it as seditious to question Article 181 matters. Penalty under Section 4(1) is up to RM5,000 fine or three years' jail. The Communications and Multimedia Act Section 233(1)(a), the Penal Code's Section 504 (insult with intent to provoke breach of peace) and Sections 298 and 298A (religious feelings, disharmony) complete the existing protective stack. The Centre for Independent Journalism recorded 233 arrests and investigations under expression-restricting laws in 2025, up from 189 in 2024. Internationally, Thailand's Penal Code Section 112 provides three to fifteen years for defaming the King, Queen, Heir-apparent or Regent. Spain (Penal Code articles 490 to 491), Denmark (Section 115, interpreted alongside the European Convention's expression clause), the Netherlands and Cambodia maintain comparable provisions. The Akta Kedaulatan Raja proposal would sit within this comparator landscape rather than create a category Malaysian law currently lacks.
sources: Federal Constitution Article 181 (AGC reprint)
Sedition Act 1948, ss.3(1)(f) and 4(1) (Federal Gazette)
Communications and Multimedia Act 1998, s.233 (Federal Gazette)
Penal Code (Act 574), ss.298, 298A, 504
Civicus Monitor, 2025 CIJ tally — 233 investigations and arrests
LawShun, Global Lèse Majesté Laws survey
Thai Penal Code s.112
ARTICLE 19, How lèse-majesté laws are eroding free speech in Southeast Asia

cards.text: The question is not whether a royal decree becomes law. It is which constitutional pathway applies — Sultan-in-Council on a state matter, Parliament with Conference of Rulers concurrence on entrenched-article amendments, Cabinet and Agong on federal bills.

cards.h: Think of it like a corporate chairman whose signature alone runs the day-to-day under the company's charter, but who needs the board's vote in writing to change the charter itself.
cards.text: The Selangor case is the chairman's signature on a chartered matter. The Akta Kedaulatan Raja proposal is a board-vote item, properly addressed to the board rather than imposed by signature.

Malaysia's constitution assigns royal authority a real sphere and elected institutions another. The Selangor pig-farming sequence honoured both halves: the Sultan acted within his Article 71 state-executive authority on a State List matter, his Exco adopted the decree on 12 February under Article 50 of the Selangor Constitution, and the licensing authority enforced. The same day the rally pressed for a new federal statute, the proposal was addressed to Parliament rather than imposed by titah. The architecture is not arrangement — it is design, and on this case the design held.

sources: Federal Constitution (AGC reprint, 1 Nov 2010); Laws of the Constitution of Selangor 1959; Sedition Act 1948; Communications and Multimedia Act 1998; Penal Code (Act 574); Child Act 2001; ISA Repeal Act 2012; Bernama; Selangor Royal Office statements 7 May and 19 May 2026; The Star; Malay Mail; FMT; Malaysiakini; CodeBlue; Business Today; NST; Kosmo Digital; Harakah Daily; Sinar Harian; The Vibes; MalaysiaNow; TRP; Buletin Mutiara; Malaysia Gazette; Civicus Monitor (CIJ data); LawShun lèse-majesté survey; ARTICLE 19 Southeast Asia analysis; Foreign Policy Association on Thai Penal Code s.112; The Malaysian Bar 'Powers and functions of state rulers'; Constitute Project Malaysia constitution; T4A brief 1980 (Zahid speech) and brief harmony-reconciliation-bill-comparator-model.md.
confidence: High — the constitutional mechanism (Article 71, Eighth Schedule, Article 50 Selangor) is primary statutory text verifiable against the AGC reprint and the Selangor state gazette. The Selangor pig-farming timeline (12 Jan, 10 Feb, 12 Feb MMKN adoption, 7 May Royal Office statement, 19 May Rukun Negara directive) is corroborated across Bernama (government wire), The Star, Malay Mail, FMT, CodeBlue, Business Today. The Daulat Tuanku rally count of 5,000 is from FMT with a Malaysiakini variance of 3,000+ noted. The Akta Kedaulatan Raja proposer (Ibrahim Ali, PUTRA) is confirmed across FMT, Malaysiakini, Harakah Daily and Kosmo; the reported provisions (detention windows, Royal Sovereignty Council) come from secondary summaries — flagged for Stage 3.
needs_verification: The exact reported provisions of the proposed Akta Kedaulatan Raja — detention without warrant for two weeks, preventive detention up to two years, Royal Sovereignty Council. Source variance across FMT, Malaysiakini, Kosmo, Harakah Daily. No draft text exists. Stage 3 should confirm the proposer's stated parameters against the Harakah Daily original (PAS organ closest to PUTRA/Ibrahim Ali) and flag any provision that comes only from secondary characterisation.

Whether the Conference of Rulers has issued any public statement on the Akta Kedaulatan Raja proposal between 16 May and the editorial close. Per T4A 3R standard, no inference is made about the Conference's view; verify the public-statement absence.

Whether any Cabinet member has publicly addressed the Akta Kedaulatan Raja proposal between 16 May and editorial close. PM Anwar's 8 May 'on hold first' framing on Bukit Tagar predates the proposal. If a Cabinet response emerges, the political-card framing needs updating.

The MMKN minute of 12 February 2026 formal adoption — Business Today and CodeBlue characterise it, but the formal MMKN release or state gazette entry would be the primary source. Stage 3 to chase if available.
Rally attendance — FMT 5,000 vs Malaysiakini 3,000+. Stage 3 should check Selangor police estimate if any was issued, and note the variance explicitly in the reader output.
source_notes: Three source-discrepancy notes for Stage 3. (1) Rally attendance: FMT reported ~5,000; Malaysiakini headline 3,000+. Card uses FMT figure with variance footnoted in the detail. (2) Akta Kedaulatan Raja provisions: characterisations across outlets describe an ISA-like template with the specific windows (2-week investigation, 2-year preventive) attributed to Ibrahim Ali's statements; the bill has no draft text in the public domain. Card phrases as 'reported provisions' and 'addressed to government, not a Cabinet bill' — both verified from multiple independent outlets including the PAS-aligned Harakah Daily. (3) Whether the Sultan's decree had legal effect on its own or only via MMKN: the brief's framing — and the cards' — is that both are true. The Sultan acted within his Article 50 / Article 71 state-executive authority; the MMKN formally adopted the position on 12 February 2026. The Sultan-in-Council mechanism is the operative state-executive pathway. The constitution does not treat these as alternatives but as the same pathway in two procedural moments. Stage 3 should confirm this is the cleanest characterisation against Malaysian Bar scholarly commentary and not over-attribute legal effect to either pole alone.
